career.notes: Per EH 1909 #530: 'an apothecary and grocer at Rutland, Vt.'

narrative_blocks.header: Apothecary and grocer at Rutland; wife predeceased him
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker was an apothecary and grocer at Rutland, Vermont, where he spent his adult life and died in 1836 at sixty-five. His wife Sarah Brown died four years before him in 1832, predeceasing him despite being nine years younger.

narrative_blocks.header: Married daughter of Col. John Brown of Pittsfield
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Brown's father, Colonel John Brown of Pittsfield, Massachusetts, is almost certainly the Revolutionary War officer killed at the Battle of Stone Arabia, New York, on October 19, 1780 — when Sarah was barely a year old. If confirmed, Thomas Hooker married the daughter of a Revolutionary War martyr.

narrative_blocks.header: Twin sons: one to Boston missions
narrative_blocks.body: John Worthington and Henry Brown Hooker were born the same day — August 31, 1802 — and their lives diverged completely: John moved to Chicago in 1836 among the city's earliest New England settlers, while Henry graduated Middlebury College, entered the ministry, and became Secretary of Missions at Boston.

narrative_blocks.header: Two of five children died in Chicago
narrative_blocks.body: John Worthington Hooker moved to Chicago in 1836 and died there in 1866; his sister Julia Ann married a Rutland lawyer and also died in Chicago in 1842. Two of Thomas Hooker's five children ended their days in the city that barely existed when they were born.

career.notes: Per EH 1909 #530: 'an apothecary and grocer at Rutland, Vt.'

narrative_blocks.header: Apothecary and grocer at Rutland; wife predeceased him
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker was an apothecary and grocer at Rutland, Vermont, where he spent his adult life and died in 1836 at sixty-five. His wife Sarah Brown died four years before him in 1832, predeceasing him despite being nine years younger.

narrative_blocks.header: Married daughter of Col. John Brown of Pittsfield
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Brown's father, Colonel John Brown of Pittsfield, Massachusetts, is almost certainly the Revolutionary War officer killed at the Battle of Stone Arabia, New York, on October 19, 1780 — when Sarah was barely a year old. If confirmed, Thomas Hooker married the daughter of a Revolutionary War martyr.

narrative_blocks.header: Twin sons: one to Boston missions
narrative_blocks.body: John Worthington and Henry Brown Hooker were born the same day — August 31, 1802 — and their lives diverged completely: John moved to Chicago in 1836 among the city's earliest New England settlers, while Henry graduated Middlebury College, entered the ministry, and became Secretary of Missions at Boston.

narrative_blocks.header: Two of five children died in Chicago
narrative_blocks.body: John Worthington Hooker moved to Chicago in 1836 and died there in 1866; his sister Julia Ann married a Rutland lawyer and also died in Chicago in 1842. Two of Thomas Hooker's five children ended their days in the city that barely existed when they were born.

narrative_blocks.header: Probably daughter of a Revolutionary War martyr
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Brown's father, Colonel John Brown of Pittsfield Massachusetts named in the Hooker genealogy, is almost certainly the Revolutionary War officer killed at the Battle of Stone Arabia, New York, on October 19, 1780 — when Sarah was barely a year old. She married Thomas Hooker in 1796 and died at Rutland, Vermont, in 1832, four years before her husband.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a Congregational minister; moved Vermont to Wisconsin
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth White Hooker married Rev. Josiah F. Goodhue in 1824 — a Middlebury College and Andover Theological Seminary graduate who served Congregational churches at Williston and Shoreham, Vermont, for twenty-four years. In 1857 they removed to Whitewater, Wisconsin, where he died in 1863 and she in January 1889 at eighty-eight.

narrative_blocks.header: Middlebury 1821, Andover Seminary
narrative_blocks.body: Josiah Goodhue graduated Middlebury College in 1821 and Andover Theological Seminary thereafter, serving as Congregational pastor at Williston and Shoreham Vermont for twenty-four years. In 1857 he and Elizabeth removed to Whitewater Wisconsin, where he died May 3, 1863.

narrative_blocks.header: Published history of Shoreham, Vermont, 1861
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Goodhue published 'History of the Town of Shoreham, Vermont, from the date of its charter, October 8th, 1761, to the present time' in 1861, printed by A.H. Copeland of Middlebury — a local history completed while he was still pastor at Shoreham. His middle name Fletcher derived from his mother's family: his mother was Elisabeth Fletcher Goodhue.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Thomas Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker Goodhue carried the Hooker family name as his middle name, honoring the lineage through his mother Elizabeth White Hooker. He continued the tradition: his son, born January 1, 1879, was also named Thomas Hooker Goodhue — the third consecutive generation to carry both names.

narrative_blocks.header: Vermont-educated merchant; spent his life in Wisconsin
narrative_blocks.body: Educated at Newton Academy in Shoreham, Vermont, Thomas Hooker Goodhue settled as a merchant at Whitewater, Wisconsin, following his parents' migration from Vermont. He died September 26, 1905 at Whitewater, where his parents and siblings had also put down roots.

cross_connections.display_label: Sister Frances died one day before her, April 8, 1895

narrative_blocks.header: Died one day after sister Frances
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Strong Goodhue died April 9, 1895, one day after her younger sister Frances Goodhue died April 8 — two sisters born nine years apart, dying within twenty-four hours of each other. Whether the deaths shared a cause is unrecorded; the timing is remarkable.

narrative_blocks.header: Twin children born 1869
narrative_blocks.body: Josiah Goodhue and Sarah Chipman had twins — Ralph Josiah and Anna Eliza — born together September 24, 1869. Anna died June 27, 1884 at about fifteen; Ralph survived. A third child, Robert Shields, was also born on September 24 — seven years later.

narrative_blocks.header: Son John Hooker died at twenty-one
narrative_blocks.body: Josiah's son John Hooker Goodhue — named for the family lineage — died August 20, 1883 at Whitewater at twenty-one years old. The name Hooker had been carried through three generations of this branch and continued in the children who survived.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived to eighty-nine in Whitewater; wife also eighty-eight
narrative_blocks.body: Josiah Goodhue died August 10, 1919 at eighty-nine years old in Wisconsin, having outlived his father Rev. Josiah Fletcher Goodhue by fifty-six years. His wife Sarah Bennett Chipman Goodhue also reached eighty-eight — both achieving remarkable longevity for the Wisconsin farming community they had settled.

cross_connections.display_label: Sister Sarah died one day after her, April 9, 1895

narrative_blocks.header: Died one day before sister Sarah
narrative_blocks.body: Frances Goodhue died April 8, 1895, one day before her elder sister Sarah Strong Goodhue died April 9 — two sisters born nine years apart, dying within twenty-four hours of each other. Whether the deaths shared a cause is unrecorded; the timing is remarkable.

career.notes: Merchant at Rutland VT per EH.
Moved to Chicago 1836 per EH. Died there 1866.

cross_connections.display_label: His twin's daughter became a missionary in South India, 1857

narrative_blocks.header: Settled Chicago in 1836; city three years old
narrative_blocks.body: John Worthington Hooker left Rutland Vermont in 1836 — the same year his father Thomas died — and moved to Chicago, then just three years old as an incorporated city, growing rapidly from a frontier trading post. He died there in 1866, having watched Chicago transform from a village of hundreds into a city approaching three hundred thousand.

narrative_blocks.header: Twin of Henry
narrative_blocks.body: John Worthington and his twin Henry Brown Hooker were born on August 31, 1802 — the same morning — and their lives diverged entirely: John to Chicago merchant life on the frontier, Henry to Congregational ministry and Boston missions. Their mother Sarah Brown died when they were twenty-nine.

narrative_blocks.header: No children recorded
narrative_blocks.body: John Worthington Hooker moved to Chicago in 1836 — the year his father Thomas died in Rutland — and spent thirty years as a merchant there until his own death in 1866. No children are documented in the Hooker genealogy, and he left no recorded descendants.

career.notes: One year as home missionary in South Carolina after ordination October 10, 1825.
May 2, 1827 to May 17, 1836 — about 10 years.
February 1837 to June 1858 — about 21 years. Obituary says 'about 10 years' — likely referring to active full-time pastorate.
Secretary 1858-1873; succeeded Rev. Dr. Clark. Continued assisting the society until death July 4, 1881.
Member Massachusetts Board of Education 1844-1851.

education.notes: Graduated Middlebury College 1821. Obituary source: Middlebury Register July 8, 1881.

education.institution_blurb: Graduated Andover Theological Seminary 1825; ordained October 10, 1825.

institutions.institution_blurb: Corporate member from 1845; daughter Sarah was ABCFM missionary in India.
American Tract Society published eight tracts from Rev. Hooker's pen.
Secretary of Massachusetts Home Missionary Society 1858-1873.

narrative_blocks.header: Secretary of Massachusetts Home Missionary Society, 1858-1873
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Henry Brown Hooker served as secretary of the Massachusetts Home Missionary Society from 1858 to 1873, succeeding Rev. Dr. Clark — capping a ministry that had included pastorates in South Carolina, Lanesboro, and Falmouth, Massachusetts. He continued assisting the society until his death on July 4, 1881, Independence Day, in Boston at seventy-eight.

narrative_blocks.header: American Tract Society published eight of his tracts
narrative_blocks.body: The American Tract Society published eight tracts from Rev. Hooker's pen, and he wrote three more for the Tract Society of Boston, as well as two Sunday-school books — 'Plea for the Heathen' and 'Put Off and Put On' — that had large sales. His obituary noted that his contributions to the religious press were 'frequent and popular.'

narrative_blocks.header: Twin who chose ministry while his brother John
narrative_blocks.body: Born the same day as his twin John Worthington Hooker on August 31, 1802, Henry took the path of Congregational ministry while John moved to Chicago's frontier in 1836. Henry's daughter Sarah Brown Hooker would carry the evangelical tradition a generation further — thirty years as a missionary to South India.

narrative_blocks.header: ABCFM corporate member from 1845
narrative_blocks.body: From 1845, Rev. Hooker served as a corporate member of the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions — the same organization his son-in-law Rev. William Banfield Capron would serve as a missionary in South India from 1857. His daughter Sarah spent thirty years at the ABCFM mission in Madura, a direct extension of her father's decades of institutional commitment.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived her husband by twelve years
narrative_blocks.body: Martha Vinal Chickering married Henry Brown Hooker on May 28, 1827 and outlived him by twelve years, dying at Roxbury, Massachusetts, November 16, 1893. She was the daughter of Asa W. and Anna Hart (Titcomb) Chickering.

notable.notable_blurb: South India missionary; Madura Girls' School founder

career.notes: Taught in district schools of Falmouth MA.
First assistant at Oliver High School Lawrence MA.
Assistant at Hartford High School (successor to Hopkins Grammar School). Met William Capron there.
In charge of Madura Girls' Boarding School on arrival 1857. Continued mission work alone 1876-1886 after husband's death. Did evangelical, medical, and educational work.
Traveled extensively giving speeches and talks about mission work.

career.organization: Bible Institute for Home and Foreign Missions (later Moody Bible Institute)
career.notes: Superintendent of Women's Department. The institute later became the Moody Bible Institute.

education.notes: Attended Wheaton Female Seminary per family history.

education.school_name: State Normal School, West Newton MA
education.notes: Attended State Normal School at West Newton MA.

institutions.institution_blurb: Teacher at Hartford High School (successor to Hopkins Grammar School).
Served under ABCFM at Madura mission 1857-1886.
Superintendent of Women's Department of the Bible Institute for Home and Foreign Missions (later Moody Bible Institute) 1889-1894.

cross_connections.display_label: Her uncle John moved to Chicago in 1836; she went to India in 1856
Husband Capron served the mission Thompson's deputation inspected

narrative_blocks.header: Ran the Madura Girls' Boarding School
narrative_blocks.body: On arriving at Madurai in 1857, Sarah Brown Hooker was immediately put in charge of the Madura Girls' Boarding School while her husband William performed evangelical work and supervised construction at Manamadurai. She had met William while both were teaching at the Hartford High School in Connecticut — she was appointed to the mission just weeks after their October 1856 marriage.

narrative_blocks.header: Her Yale missionary husband died in India, 1876
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. William Banfield Capron died of heart disease at Madura on October 6, 1876, leaving Sarah a widow with two surviving daughters far from Massachusetts — Annie and Laura remained in the United States under the care of Sarah's sister Anne. He was fifty-two; they had been married twenty years.

narrative_blocks.header: Remained at the mission alone for ten years
narrative_blocks.body: After William's death in 1876, Sarah Brown Hooker chose to remain at the Madurai mission alone — doing evangelical, medical, and educational work in Manamadurai and Madurai until 1886, a decade of solitary service in South India. She finally returned to the United States after thirty years abroad.

narrative_blocks.header: Superintendent at the Bible Institute that became Moody's
narrative_blocks.body: In 1889, Dwight L. Moody personally asked Sarah to become superintendent of the Women's Department at his new Bible Institute in Chicago; when she questioned her fitness, he replied, 'It is the experience of life that I want.' She served until 1894, then retired to Boston, conducted a Bible class at Old South Church, and eventually died in Poughkeepsie on December 15, 1918 at ninety.

marriages.notes: Arthur Webster Tufts, son of Gilbert and Mary (Chickering) Tufts of Charlestown MA. Died April 1892. After widowhood, Anne cared for the Capron daughters (Annie H. and Laura E.) while Sarah returned to India 1874. Later lived in Poughkeepsie with Laura and James Dyer Keith.

narrative_blocks.header: Cared for the missionary nieces while Sarah was
narrative_blocks.body: When Sarah Brown Hooker and William Capron returned to India in 1874, their daughters Annie and Laura remained in the United States under the care of Anne Elizabeth Tufts and her husband Arthur Webster Tufts. After her husband died in 1892, Anne lived with her sister Sarah, and eventually both women moved to Poughkeepsie to live with Laura; Anne died there April 9, 1917, and Sarah died there in 1918.

burial.plot_notes: Forest Hills Cemetery and Crematory, Jamaica Plain, Suffolk County MA. FGA #38209990.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at two years old in Falmouth
narrative_blocks.body: Martha Vinal Chickering Hooker was born January 1, 1836 and died March 6, 1838 in Falmouth, Massachusetts — just over two years old, named for her mother Martha Vinal Chickering Hooker. She was the third child of the Rev. Henry Brown Hooker and his wife Martha.

narrative_blocks.header: Born 1806; died 1830 at twenty-four
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Strong Hooker was born in 1806 at Rutland, the fourth of five children, and died in 1830 at about twenty-four — surviving her twin brothers by childhood and predeceasing her younger sister Julia. No spouse or children are recorded in the Hooker genealogy.

narrative_blocks.header: Married the Vermont Adjutant General; died in Chicago
narrative_blocks.body: Julia Ann Hooker married Frederick William Hopkins — who served as Adjutant and Inspector General of Vermont and compiled the state's military laws — in December 1836, the same year her brother John moved to Chicago. She died in Chicago September 27, 1842, one of two Thomas Hooker children to die in that city.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty-three; daughter Sarah Hooker survived her
narrative_blocks.body: Julia Ann died in Chicago at about thirty-three in 1842, leaving a daughter Sarah Hooker Hopkins — whose middle name Hooker honored the family line — who was just five years old. Her husband Frederick survived her and continued his Vermont legal career.

burial.plot_notes: West Street Cemetery, Rutland VT. FGA #. She died in Chicago but was buried in the family cemetery in Rutland.

career.notes: Long-time Rutland Justice of the Peace.
Vermont Adjutant and Inspector General 1837-1852. Worked with Franklin Pierce, Alden Partridge, Truman B. Ransom in militia reform.
Earned accolades leaving sick bed to save court records during courthouse fire April 3, 1868.
Organized Rutland Union School District 1855. Also organized Rutland's first volunteer fire department.

narrative_blocks.header: Compiled Vermont's military laws; state adjutant general
narrative_blocks.body: Frederic Williams Hopkins graduated Middlebury College, practiced law as clerk of Rutland County Court, and rose to Adjutant and Inspector General of the State of Vermont, compiling the state's military laws. He married Julia Ann Hooker in December 1836, the same month her brother John moved to Chicago.

narrative_blocks.header: He clerked the county courts for years
narrative_blocks.body: A Middlebury graduate, Hopkins practiced law at Rutland and long served as clerk of the Rutland County courts. His grave was vandalized in 1891 and his remains moved to Evergreen Cemetery.

burial.plot_notes: Originally buried West Street Cemetery CEM553; remains moved to Evergreen Cemetery after 1891 grave vandalism.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Hooker; mother Julia died when she was
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hooker Hopkins was born November 17, 1837, her middle name Hooker honoring her mother Julia Ann Hooker's family line. Her mother died in Chicago in September 1842 when Sarah was just four years old, leaving her father Frederick Hopkins to raise her.

narrative_blocks.header: Settled Monte Vista, Colorado
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hooker Hopkins and Josiah Perkins Woodbury settled at Monte Vista in Rio Grande County, Colorado — far from Rutland, Vermont, where she was born. Two of their six children died very young: Annie Lawrence at four months, Jean Hopkins at four years.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband was treasurer of the Panama Railroad
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hooker Hopkins married Joseph Perkins Woodbury — a Vermont lawyer who had served as treasurer of the Panama Railroad on the Isthmus — in Rutland on November 19, 1861. They moved to Monte Vista, Colorado, then eventually to Los Angeles, where Sarah died January 23, 1915, and where Joseph was buried after his death in 1895.

burial.plot_notes: Forest Lawn Memorial Park, Glendale, Los Angeles County CA. FGA #235629853.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at about two
narrative_blocks.body: Martha Vinal Hopkins was born about 1840 and died February 16, 1843, at about two years old — the second of Julia Ann Hooker's two children to outlive their mother, who had died in Chicago the previous year. Her first name honored Martha Vinal Chickering, wife of her uncle Henry Brown Hooker.

ministry_positions.notes: Ordained Dec 5, 1753; settled there 24 years until death by smallpox

primary_residence.notes: Minister here 24 years 1753-1777. Yale 1751, ordained Dec 5, 1753.

narrative_blocks.header: Took Jonathan Edwards' Northampton pulpit, died of smallpox
narrative_blocks.body: Ordained in 1753, he became pastor at Northampton, Massachusetts — the church where Jonathan Edwards had preached until his dismissal in 1750. He held that pulpit for nearly 25 years, dying of smallpox on February 6, 1777, in his 48th year. His was the most prominent pastoral appointment held by any Hooker descendant of his generation.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife's father, Colonel Worthington, named Worthington Parish
narrative_blocks.body: He married Sarah Worthington, daughter of Colonel Worthington of Springfield, Massachusetts — the man who gave his name to Worthington Parish in Berlin, Connecticut, the parish where the Hart and Hooker families had long been prominent. The marriage connected the Hooker line to one of western Massachusetts's most distinguished families.

narrative_blocks.header: Smallpox took the Northampton pastor at forty-seven
narrative_blocks.body: He served as pastor of the Northampton MA Congregational Church from 1753 until smallpox killed him in February 1777 at forty-seven. His daughter Sarah Hooker married the Northampton lawyer Caleb Strong nine months later — passing the Hooker name into the family of a future Massachusetts Governor and U.S. Senator.

cross_connections.display_label: Edwards's daughter refused his pulpit and her chair sat in the vestibule.
His niece's son Abel Hollister came under his guardianship

narrative_blocks.header: Named for a brother who died nine days
narrative_blocks.body: His older brother John was born and died in December 1693 — living only nine days. When the next son arrived in 1695, his parents gave him the same name. He carried it through 71 years, Justice of the Peace and prominent Kensington businessman.

narrative_blocks.header: He and his three sisters all married into
narrative_blocks.body: His sisters Abigail, Mary, and Sarah married three sons of Capt. John Hart between 1719 and 1725. John himself had already married Mercy Hart in 1728 — from a different Hart branch, through Dea. Ebenezer Hart. Four Hooker siblings, two separate Hart lines, one generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Justice of the Peace
narrative_blocks.body: EH describes him as "a prominent and active man in the business of the town." He held the office of Justice of the Peace in Kensington, a parish of Berlin CT, and died there on August 3, 1766, age 71.

parents.father_research_notes: EH says father was Deacon Thomas Hart — WRONG. Barbour Collection Farmington VR confirms father was Dea. Ebenezer Hart (X00119). Primary source wins.

sources.notes: Barbour Collection confirms: father = Dea. Ebenezer Hart

bio.notes: EH gives 'Mercy (Mary) Hart' — both names used interchangeably

burialCemetery.name: West Street Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Rutland Rutland County Vermont. Contains H00530 Dr. Thomas Hooker (1770-1836) and I01188 Sarah Brown Hooker (1779-1832). FGA Cemetery #824620.
